oob.text:
I'll dig into this for you. Tiny houses in Portland have some specific rules, and the answer depends heavily on your lot's zoning, so let me check both the general regulations and your specific property.Now let me look up the specific zoning and characteristics of this property.Let me try to pull up the specific property on PortlandMaps directly.Lots of neighbors on this street are R7 zoning. Let me verify the specific property.Let me try to fetch the specific Redfin listing for this address directly.Confirmed the property: 1 bed/1 bath, 960 sqft, built 1947. Now let me check the specific R7 zone requirements for ADUs.Short answer: **Yes, almost certainly** — but the most useful path depends on whether you want a permanent structure or something faster and lighter. Let me lay out what I found, with a few things you probably don't know that change the calculus.

## What your specific lot looks like

Your property at 831 SE 174th Ave is a 1 bed/1 bath, 960 sqft single-family home built in 1947. Every neighboring property I could check on SE 174th in 97233 is **R7-zoned** (1142, 1621, 1711, 1772, 1415, 2150 SE 174th all show R7), and Portland's official zoning code groups your neighborhood (Centennial/Hazelwood) into R7. I'd put high confidence on R7 for your lot, but **verify it yourself** at portlandmaps.com before spending a dime on design. R7 is one of the most ADU-friendly zones in Portland.

## Three real paths, ranked by speed-to-livable

**Path 1: Tiny house on wheels (THOW) parked on your lot — fastest, lightest**

This is a path most people don't realize exists. Portland treats tiny houses on wheels as "occupied recreational vehicles" under Chapter 33.260 of the Portland Zoning Code, and you're allowed one on a lot that already has a house. No building permit for the structure itself. You'll need an electrical permit for a dedicated 20-amp GFCI circuit/pedestal connection and a plumbing permit if you connect to sewer (or you can use the main house's bathroom). Enforcement is complaint-driven. The DMV may register the unit as a camper, travel trailer, or park model RV. This path is days-to-weeks, not months.

**Path 2: Detached ADU (a "tiny house on a foundation") — the standard play**

This is what most people mean by building a tiny house. Portland is genuinely one of the most ADU-friendly cities in the U.S. Your specific numbers:

- **Max ADU size**: 75% of your main house's living area, OR 800 sqft, whichever is smaller. With your 960 sqft home, **your cap is ~720 sqft**. That's a true tiny-house footprint.
- **Max height**: 20 ft outside setbacks; 15 ft within setbacks (with 10 ft max wall height).
- **Setbacks**: Detached ADUs must sit at least 40 ft back from the front lot line, or behind the rear wall of your house.
- **Lot size**: R7 minimum is 7,000 sqft for one home, but for ADU purposes the threshold is 5,000 sqft, which essentially every lot in your area meets.
- **Parking**: None required.
- **Number allowed**: R7 actually allows **up to two ADUs** if the lot meets the size minimum and fronts a city-maintained street (174th does). Most homeowners don't know this.

**Path 3: Convert an existing structure (garage, basement, attic)**

If your lot has a detached garage or shed, converting it is usually the cheapest, fastest permanent option. Same size limits apply, but you skip new-construction costs.

## The money-saver almost nobody mentions

Portland has an **SDC (System Development Charges) Waiver** that can save several thousand dollars in fees if you commit to using the ADU as long-term residential rental (or owner-occupied) for at least 10 years. The catch: you record a covenant agreeing not to use it as a short-term rental (Airbnb/VRBO) for that 10-year period. If renting on Airbnb is part of your plan, the waiver is off the table.

## Three free things to do before you spend any money

1. **Free 15-minute call with a Portland city planner.** Anyone can book this on portland.gov; they'll confirm your zone, overlays, and any catches specific to your lot. Cheapest mistake-avoidance ever.
2. **Pull your permit history on PortlandMaps.** If a previous owner did unpermitted work (added a bathroom, finished a basement, built a shed over 200 sqft), the city will require you to permit that work *as part of* your ADU project. This is the single biggest source of nasty surprises.
3. **Download the Detached Covered Accessory Structures Worksheet** from portland.gov (it's a 1.29 MB PDF). It walks through every dimensional rule for your zone in a single document.

## Things to verify on your specific lot

- **Lot size** (R7 ADU needs 5,000+ sqft; your block looks like 7,000–14,000 sqft properties, but check yours)
- **Overlay zones** ('z' constrained-site, 'c' environmental conservation, 'p' environmental protection) — these can kill or reshape an ADU project. Your neighborhood is mostly clear of these but verify
- **Sewer connection requirements** — older homes sometimes need upgraded sewer lines for an additional unit; this can add $5–15k
- **Stormwater management** — adding impervious surface (a new building) may trigger requirements
- **Permit history gaps** on the 1947 house

## A few things to be cautious about

You'll see lots of third-party "ADU builders" and "buildability scorers" online. Some are good; some are SEO bait that wants your contact info. The official rules live at portland.gov and portlandmaps.com — start there. If you talk to a builder, they should be able to cite the specific Title 33 chapter (33.205 for ADUs) for any claim they make.

Also: an ADU **will increase your property's assessed value**, and therefore your property taxes. Multnomah County determines the bump, not the city. Worth modeling before you commit.

---

If it'd help, I could pull the Title 33 Chapter 33.205 PDF text and write up a one-page checklist tailored to a 720-sqft detached ADU on an R7 lot — basically a "what to hand a designer/builder on day one" doc. Or if you're leaning toward the tiny-house-on-wheels path, I could pull together the exact electrical/plumbing permit specs Portland requires. Either one's a good next step.

mcp.text:
I have the property data. Now let me check Portland's current tiny house and ADU regulations.Let me get specifics on R7 zone setbacks and the Aircraft Landing overlay since both apply here.Let me verify the current SDC waiver status and what the "h" Aircraft Landing overlay means for this site.Yes, you can build a tiny house at 831 SE 174th Ave, and your lot is actually a strong candidate for it. But before the answer: a quick flag worth raising upfront.

**Heads up on ownership.** Multnomah County records currently show this property owned by Karam & Janoura, with a 2020 sale price of $240K. If you're researching it as a potential acquisition rather than a property you already own, the constraints below would transfer to whoever owns it. If you've recently bought it and the assessor just hasn't updated yet, ignore this note.

## What you've got

Your lot is **0.19 acres (~8,276 sq ft)** with a **960 sq ft house from 1947** in the Centennial neighborhood. The zoning is **R7 (Residential 7,000)** with an **"h" Aircraft Landing overlay** (related to Troutdale Airport flight paths; for a single-story tiny house this almost certainly won't bind, but verify the specific height ceiling at this location with a planner). No flood, landslide, or wildfire hazard zones; sewer through City of Portland BES; water through Rockwood Water PUD (not Portland Water Bureau, which matters for utility connection planning).

## Three real paths, in plain terms

**Path 1: Detached ADU on a foundation (most common "tiny house" path).** This is the cleanest legal route. Because you already have a primary house, R7 imposes no minimum lot size for a single ADU; you just need frontage on a city-maintained street (174th qualifies). Specific limits for your lot:

- Max ADU living area: **720 sq ft** (75% of your 960 sq ft house, since that's smaller than the 800 sq ft cap)
- Max height: 20 ft outside setbacks, 15 ft within setbacks
- Must be either ≥40 ft from the front lot line or located behind the existing house
- No additional parking required
- Combined accessory structure footprint capped at 15% of lot (≈1,240 sq ft)

**Path 2: Two detached ADUs.** Since 2021 (RIP1), R7 lots ≥5,000 sq ft can have **two ADUs** alongside the primary house. You're at 8,276 sq ft, so you qualify. Same 720 sq ft / 20 ft / siting rules per unit, and one of the two must meet visitability standards (no-step entry, accessible bathroom, ≥200 sq ft accessible living area).

**Path 3: Tiny house on wheels (THOW).** Portland treats THOWs as RVs, not buildings. One occupied THOW is allowed on a residential lot that already has a primary dwelling. It must remain mobile (wheels stay on), parked ≥5 ft from the house, and have a dedicated 20-amp GFCI electrical hookup. Cannot be a short-term rental. This is the fastest, cheapest path but the unit doesn't add appraised value or count toward density.

There are also wilder options your lot could technically support (cottage cluster needs ≥7,000 sq ft in R7 — you qualify; up to 16 detached units, no ADUs allowed in that path), but those are full development plays, not "tiny house" scope.

## The thing most people miss right now

Portland City Council passed Ordinance 192082 in July 2025, creating a **temporary SDC exemption window for new housing units running Aug 15, 2025 through Sept 30, 2028**. SDCs (system development charges) typically run $15K–$30K+ on a new ADU. There are two stacking programs:

- The long-running **ADU SDC Waiver** (10-year covenant, no short-term rental) — still active, no expiration
- The **temporary citywide SDC exemption** for new dwelling units — different program, runs through Sept 2028

If you're going to build, the 2026–2028 window is meaningfully cheaper than building post-2028. Don't sit on this if it's actually on your roadmap.

## Practical next steps

The single highest-leverage move is the **free 15-minute planner consultation** through Portland Permitting & Development at 503-823-7300, or an "Early Assistance" meeting for deeper review. Bring the property ID **R337949** and state ID **1S3E06AB 8800**. Ask specifically: (1) the exact height ceiling imposed by the "h" overlay at this lot, (2) whether the existing 1947 house has any unpermitted additions that could complicate ADU permitting (the property had only one bathroom of record, worth verifying), and (3) sewer connection requirements since BES may require a separate or upsized lateral.

For permits, you'll use **Development Hub PDX** online. Note that you cannot do the electrical work yourself on an ADU even as the homeowner; that requires a licensed electrical contractor. The general construction work you can DIY with the Owner Doing Work form.

---

Two things I can do from here if useful: I can save a structured summary of this research (with the property IDs, code section references, and SDC links) into your Obsidian vault under `project-docs/` so it's there next time you pick this up, and/or I can pull the actual permit history for this address to check whether the existing house has any open or unresolved permits that would complicate things. Want either or both?

enhanced.text:
I'm going to give this the depth it deserves. Before drafting anything, I want to pull the actual data on your specific lot rather than give you generic Portland advice — that way the answer is anchored to your property, not a hypothetical one. Let me load the Portland Maps tools and look up your address.I pulled the actual data on your specific lot before answering, so the rules below are anchored to what's recorded for 831 SE 174th Ave (Property ID R337949) rather than generic Portland advice. A few things to flag up front:

**Property snapshot (from PortlandMaps and city assessor records)**

- **Base zone:** R7 ("Residential 7,000"); Comp Plan designation also R7.
- **Overlay:** "h" Aircraft Landing Overlay only.
- **No plan district, no historic/conservation district, no environmental overlay (c or p), no design overlay.**
- **Lot:** 0.19 acres (~8,276 sqft); existing house 960 sqft, built 1947.
- **Watershed/sewer basin:** Johnson Creek (North Powell Butte subwatershed). Property is connected to City sanitary sewer (8" PVC main in SE 174th Ave; your 6" lateral connects ~11 ft south of a manhole at ~6 ft depth, per BES records). Storm drainage uses sumps in your area.
- **Hazards:** NOT in FEMA flood, NOT in mapped landslide hazard, NOT in liquefaction zone, ~2% slope, soil hydrologic group B (well-drained).
- **Special:** Eligible for the East 205 Urban Renewal Area; flagged as a "Low/Moderate Income Neighborhood" (matters for some grant programs); NOT eligible for the Holte property tax exemption.
- **Public records note:** the assessor lists ownership as "KARAM, CHAFEK & JANOURA, ANESA" with a 2020-04-09 sale date. If you've purchased it more recently than that, county records may simply not have caught up yet, but worth a sanity check before you spend money on plans.

Now to your questions, with code citations and plain-English translation. I will mark anywhere I am unsure, because Title 33 gets amended at least twice a year and I want you to verify the live text before relying on any number.

---

## 1. What kind of "tiny house" are you actually allowed to build?

Portland recognizes three legally distinct things. They are not interchangeable.

**A. Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) on a permanent foundation** — *Title 33, Chapter 33.205*

This is what most people picture when they say "backyard tiny house." It is a real dwelling with a foundation, full Oregon Residential Specialty Code (ORSC) construction, and a Certificate of Occupancy. ADUs are allowed in R7 by right (one ADU on a lot with a single primary dwelling; recent code consolidations in 33.205 also opened a path to two ADUs in some configurations, see 33.205.040.C).

- **Size cap:** 800 sqft of living area, OR 75% of the primary dwelling's living area, whichever is less (33.205.040.C). **For your lot this matters: with a 960 sqft existing house, your ADU is capped at 720 sqft, not 800.** Garages and storage don't count.
- **Height:** 20 ft outside required setbacks; 15 ft inside setbacks (33.205.040.C and 33.110.245).
- **Use:** Can be your primary residence, family member's home, or a long-term rental. Type A and Type B short-term rentals (Airbnb-style) are technically allowed under 33.207 and 33.815.040.B.1, BUT if you take the SDC waiver (see Section 5), you sign a 10-year covenant prohibiting STR use of any structure on the lot.

**B. Tiny house on wheels (THOW) / RV-classed** — *Title 33, Chapter 33.260 (definitions) and Section 33.266.150 (parking)*

Portland is unusually permissive here. Since the August 2021 "Shelter to Housing Continuum" code update, one occupied RV or THOW is allowed as long-term housing on a developed residential lot that already has a primary dwelling.

- **Treated as an "occupied recreational vehicle," not a dwelling.** No building permit for the unit itself; ORSC does not apply because it's a vehicle (Oregon DMV registers it as a camper, travel trailer, or park-model RV).
- **The wheels must stay on.** Removing them converts it into an unpermitted structure.
- **Placement:** behind the front-facing facade of the primary house; cannot be in a front yard; minimum 5 ft from primary dwelling per the city's published rules.
- **Hookups:** you DO need permits for the utility connections (campground-style RV power pedestal, water connection, sewer dump similar to a cleanout). Those are Title 25 (plumbing), Title 26 (electrical), and BES sewer.
- **Use:** Long-term occupancy and long-term rental are allowed. **Short-term rental is prohibited** under this provision.
- **Cannot be on a vacant lot.** You must already have a permitted house.

**C. "Tiny home" as a primary dwelling on a foundation**

Portland doesn't have a "tiny home" zoning category. If it's a permanent dwelling on a foundation, it's regulated as a single-family house under Title 33.110 and Title 24 building code, regardless of size. It must meet ORSC, including ORSC Appendix Q (the tiny home appendix, allowing dwellings ≤400 sqft of floor area to use ladder/loft accommodations and reduced ceiling heights). On your lot you cannot replace your existing house with a tiny home under R7 minimum-density rules without careful planning, but you could build one as the primary on a vacant lot or via a middle-housing land division (33.644).

**Quick comparison for your specific situation:**

| Option | On-foundation? | Building permit? | Max size | STR allowed? | Long-term rental? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ADU (33.205) | Yes | Full set | 720 sqft (you) | Yes if no SDC waiver; No for 10 yrs if waiver | Yes |
| THOW (33.260/.266) | No | Hookups only | DMV/RV size limits (8.5 ft wide) | No | Yes |
| Detached primary house | Yes | Full set | Subject to FAR/coverage | N/A | Yes |

---

## 2. Zoning of 831 SE 174th Ave and what it allows

**Base zone: R7 (Single-Dwelling 7,000)** under Chapter 33.110. The R7 standards are in Table 110-3 (lots), Table 110-4 (development standards), and Table 110-5 (building coverage). I'm citing the framework here; verify the live numbers because 33.110 has been amended multiple times since 2021 (Residential Infill Project, Shelter to Housing Continuum, ezone corrections). Typical R7 standards as of the last published code packet:

- **Minimum lot area:** 7,000 sqft (your lot is ~8,276 sqft, so you clear this).
- **Minimum lot width and depth:** see Table 110-3.
- **Maximum height:** 35 ft (base R7); your "h" overlay does NOT further restrict this for residential zones (per 33.400.020.B, in R, C, IR, and CI1 zones the base zone height applies, NOT the overlay's airspace surface). So the aircraft overlay is essentially a non-issue for your build.
- **FAR (floor area ratio):** Table 110-4. R7 has a base FAR around 0.4 with a "preserving existing dwelling" bonus available if your existing 1947 house was last finaled at least 5 years ago AND you alter ≤25% of its street-facing facade.
- **Building coverage:** Table 110-5 (typically 50% for R7 lots in this size range; verify).
- **Setbacks:** Table 110-4. For R7 generally: 10 ft front, 5 ft side, 10 ft rear. Confirm; setback rules also have garage-entrance and street-side variants.
- **Minimum density:** 33.110.210. R7 has a minimum dwelling unit density on certain new-development sites (since the Residential Infill Project), but this generally applies when you're proposing brand-new development without preserving an existing primary dwelling. Adding an ADU to your existing house does not trigger it.

**Overlay: "h" Aircraft Landing** (Chapter 33.400). For your residential use, this is informational only; height is governed by R7's 35 ft limit. No special review.

**No plan district.** This is a clean lot from a regulatory complexity standpoint, which is good news.

**More than one dwelling unit allowed?** Yes. Under the **Residential Infill Project** (effective Aug 1, 2021), R7 (along with R5 and R2.5) now allows **up to 4 units per lot by right** (duplex, triplex, fourplex), and **up to 6 units** if at least 50% are affordable at 60% MFI (33.110.240, "Density"; also Table 110-2 for housing types). Standards differ from a house+ADU combo (FAR is calculated for the whole site; there are massing and design standards specific to multi-unit configurations). **This is the surprising one for you:** if your goal is a "tiny house" purely as backyard rental income, an ADU is the simple path; but if you're open to a duplex or triplex, the temporary SDC exemption (Section 5 below) currently makes a multi-unit build cheaper to permit than it has been in a long time.

---

## 3. Permits and the typical sequence

Plain-English order of operations for an ADU on your lot:

1. **Property research & free planner appointment** (no permit, but essential). PortlandMaps for zoning; 15-minute free city planner appointment via 503-823-7300. This is where you'd confirm setbacks, FAR, and ask about RIP options.
2. **Optional: Pre-Permit Zoning Plan Check or Early Assistance** (Land Use Services, ~$300-1,500). Gives you a written zoning summary tied to a draft site plan before you spend money on full architectural drawings. *Strongly recommend this for you* given you have no construction background.
3. **Building permit application** (Title 24 — Bureau of Development Services, now part of Portland Permitting & Development / PP&D). For an ADU this is filed online via DevHub PDX. Includes site plan, floor plans, structural, energy, mechanical compliance.
4. **Trade permits** (file with the building permit OR later):
   - **Plumbing** — Title 25, PP&D
   - **Electrical** — Title 26, PP&D
   - **Mechanical/HVAC** — Title 27, PP&D
5. **Sewer connection / capacity review** — Bureau of Environmental Services (BES). On your lot, your 6" PVC lateral is already to the city main. An ADU may share or get its own connection; BES will tell you whether your existing lateral has capacity or needs upsizing.
6. **Stormwater** — BES Site Development. Your area uses sumps and your lot has small impervious area; you'll likely qualify for **Simplified Approach** in the BES Stormwater Management Manual (the simpler path), but confirm.
7. **Water service** — Portland Water Bureau. Determine separate vs. shared meter.
8. **Driveway / curb cut** — PBOT, only if you're adding a new curb cut. For most backyard ADUs you reuse the existing.
9. **Tree review** — Title 11 (Urban Forestry, Parks). Triggered if any regulated tree (generally ≥6" DBH on private property; ≥12" for some species; AND any street tree of any size) is within the construction impact zone. You will need a Title 11 permit to remove or significantly impact any regulated tree.
10. **Fire** — Title 31. Fire access is usually only an issue if your ADU is on a flag lot or >150 ft from a public street; otherwise standard residential rules apply.
11. **Inspections during construction**, then **final inspection / Certificate of Occupancy**.

**Land use review tier — could you trigger Type II or III?** For a standard ADU meeting all 33.205 standards on your R7 lot, NO; it's permit-only (Type I-equivalent zoning check folded into the building permit). You'd trigger:
- **Adjustment Review (Type II under 33.805)** if you can't meet a development standard and need to reduce a setback, exceed coverage, etc. ~8-10 weeks of review.
- **Environmental Review (Type II/III under 33.430)** — NOT applicable on your lot (no "p" or "c" overlay).
- **Historic Resource Review** — NOT applicable (no historic district).
- **Land Division / Middle Housing Land Division (33.671)** — only if you're splitting the lot.

A pre-application conference is REQUIRED for Type III/IV reviews; it's optional but recommended for Type I or II. For your project it shouldn't be required.

---

## 4. Site-specific gotchas (anchored to your actual lot data)

- **Trees (Title 11):** I cannot tell remotely whether you have regulated trees in the build zone. Walk the lot and identify every tree ≥6" DBH; any that are within ~10 ft of where you'd build need a tree-impact assessment. Street trees in the SE 174th right-of-way are fully protected. Permit a tree removal/preservation review through Urban Forestry early; it can take several weeks and you can't pull a building permit until the tree disposition is settled.
- **Stormwater:** Good news. Your soil group is B, slope is ~2%, and your area drains via city sumps (not a creek). You'll almost certainly qualify for the **Simplified Approach** in the BES Stormwater Management Manual. A small ADU adds little impervious area, so onsite infiltration (a drywell or planter) should be straightforward.
- **Sewer:** You're on city sewer. The 8" PVC main runs in SE 174th and your 6" lateral exists. **System Development Charges (SDCs)** still apply unless waived/exempted (see Section 5). The BES sanitary SDC and the stormwater SDC are separate line items.
- **Floodplain / landslide / environmental zones:** None of these apply to your lot. Despite being in the Johnson Creek watershed, your specific parcel is not in any FEMA flood zone, landslide hazard, environmental conservation/protection overlay, or PIR Plan District. This removes about 80% of the gotchas that catch east-Portland builders by surprise.
- **Setback from existing house to ADU:** 6 ft separation is the typical minimum between primary dwelling and detached accessory (33.110.250 — verify; this has been adjusted recently). For a THOW it's 5 ft.
- **Parking:** Portland has eliminated the off-street parking requirement for ADUs across the board (33.266 amendments and Oregon HB 2001 / 2003). You do not need to add a parking space for an ADU at this address.
- **Utilities:** Your ADU may share or have separate water/sewer/electric. Separate is more flexible if you ever rent it out. **A new address will be assigned** by BDS once the permit is issued; ask early so you can give it to utility companies.
- **Foundation requirement:** Yes, an ADU REQUIRES a permanent foundation to count as a dwelling under the building code. A non-foundation tiny home becomes a THOW under 33.260 and is a different legal animal entirely.

---

## 5. Cost and timeline reality check

**System Development Charges (SDCs)** are the big budget line. For a typical Portland ADU built around 2024-2025, total SDCs (Water + BES Sanitary + BES Stormwater + Parks + Transportation) ran roughly $12,000 to $19,000 depending on size and impact. **Two important programs may apply to you right now:**

1. **ADU SDC Waiver** (Title 17.13/17.14, administered by Revenue Division). Active and indefinite. You sign a 10-year covenant: no STR use of any structure on the property. Waives nearly all SDCs. Penalty for violation: 150% of current SDCs at the time of violation. Email aducovenant@portlandoregon.gov.
2. **Temporary SDC Exemption Program** (Portland City Code 17.14.070 Section J, ordinance 192082, July 2025). Covers permits issued **Aug 15, 2025 through Sept 30, 2028**. Exempts new dwelling units from SDCs. **Important caveat: this exemption explicitly EXCLUDES ADUs and caretaker quarters.** It applies to *new dwelling units* (think: duplex, triplex, fourplex via RIP, or a new primary dwelling). So if you go the ADU route, you use the older ADU SDC Waiver. If you go the duplex/triplex route via RIP, the temporary exemption applies and there's no STR covenant attached.

**Permit fees** for an ADU (separate from SDCs) typically run $6,000-$10,000 across building, plumbing, electrical, mechanical. Fee schedule: portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules.

**Timelines** (typical, not guaranteed):
- Pre-permit early assistance: 3 weeks for written response after intake
- Plan review: 4-8 weeks for over-the-counter or simple ADU; 8-20 weeks for new detached ADU including any correction cycles
- Construction: 4-8 months for a detached ADU with a competent contractor
- Total project: realistically plan on **9-18 months from "I'm starting" to occupancy**

**Common reasons projects stall in plan review:**
1. Tree review not started early enough (single biggest scheduler)
2. Setback or FAR miscalculations on the site plan
3. Missing structural details (foundation, lateral load path) for new construction
4. BES stormwater path picked wrong (Simplified vs. Performance)
5. Energy code (Oregon Energy Efficiency Specialty Code) compliance documentation

---

## 6. Who to call, in order

1. **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D), Development Services Center** — main number **503-823-7300** (Mon-Fri 8 AM-5 PM). 1900 SW 4th Ave, Suite 5000, Portland OR 97201. In-person Tuesdays 8-4 and Thursdays 8-noon, by appointment. **Free 15-minute zoning appointment** is your no-cost first step.
2. **Land Use Services intake** for early assistance / pre-app: **landuseintake@portlandoregon.gov**
3. **Bureau of Environmental Services (BES) Site Development & Stormwater** — through PP&D's main line, or at portland.gov/bes. They handle sewer connection, stormwater management plan review, and System Development Charge calculations for sanitary/stormwater.
4. **Portland Water Bureau** — 503-823-7770. Water service, meter sizing, water SDC.
5. **Urban Forestry (Portland Parks & Recreation, Title 11 administration)** — 503-823-TREE (8733). Tree permits and street-tree impact reviews. Required if any tree ≥6" DBH is in your build zone or on the SE 174th right-of-way.
6. **PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation)** — 503-823-5185, only if you're adding/altering a curb cut.
7. **Portland Fire & Rescue (Title 31)** — through PP&D fire reviewer; typically only contacted directly for >150 ft setback or unusual access situations.
8. **ADU SDC Waiver questions** — aducovenant@portlandoregon.gov (Revenue Division)

**Yes, request the free pre-permit zoning appointment first.** Even if you ultimately need a paid Pre-Permit Zoning Plan Check ($300-1,500), the free 15-minute slot is enough to confirm your basic concept is allowed before you hire an architect or designer.

---

## 7. Plain-language next-steps checklist

1. **Pull up your property on PortlandMaps** (portlandmaps.com → search 831 SE 174th Ave). Click the Zoning, Environmental, and Permits tabs. Verify what I've summarized; if anything has changed since today (May 4, 2026), trust the live record.
2. **Confirm you actually own the property as currently recorded.** The county assessor still lists Karam/Janoura. If you've purchased or are about to, tie that down before paying the city for anything.
3. **Walk the lot and inventory trees.** Note every tree ≥6" diameter at breast height (4.5 ft up the trunk). Photograph street trees in the SE 174th right-of-way too. This is your single highest-leverage early action.
4. **Decide your goal: ADU vs. THOW vs. duplex/triplex (RIP).** If you want a backyard rental for income or a family member, ADU is simplest. If you want low cost / fast move-in for yourself or a tenant, THOW is dramatically faster and cheaper. If you want maximum value creation and the temporary SDC exemption window, RIP duplex/triplex is the play.
5. **Schedule the free 15-min city planner appointment** (call 503-823-7300). Bring your address, a rough site sketch, and your top option from step 4. Ask: "What setbacks, FAR, and coverage apply on R7h? Are there any minimum-density triggers I'd hit? Is the SDC exemption available for a new dwelling unit on this lot?"
6. **Request Early Assistance / Pre-Permit Zoning Plan Check** (email landuseintake@portlandoregon.gov) once your concept is sharper. ~3-week turnaround. This catches problems before drawings.
7. **Get a survey** if your lot lines aren't already documented from a recent transaction. Setbacks measure from property lines, and a 6" error on a 5 ft side setback costs you the build.
8. **Hire a designer or architect familiar with Portland ADU permitting.** Even simple ADU projects benefit from someone who has been through DevHub PDX recently. Portland publishes free **pre-approved ADU plan sets** worth considering (portland.gov/ppd; search "pre-approved ADU plans").
9. **Apply for a Tree Permit (Title 11)** as soon as you know which trees you need to impact. Don't wait until building permit submittal.
10. **If pursuing the ADU SDC Waiver:** download the application/covenant PDF from portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/adu-sdc-waiver, sign it early, and submit to the Revenue Division while plan review is happening. (Or, if pursuing a duplex/triplex, submit the Temporary SDC Exemption form per portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules/housing-sdc-exemption.)
11. **Submit building permit + trade permits via DevHub PDX.** Pay fees at issuance.
12. **Schedule inspections in sequence:** foundation/concrete, framing, plumbing rough-in, electrical rough-in, mechanical rough-in, insulation, drywall, final.
13. **Schedule the final inspection.** Get the Certificate of Occupancy. Update your address records, set up utility accounts, and (if renting) document move-in condition.

---

## 8. Accuracy caveats — what I cannot verify remotely and what to confirm

I want to be straight with you about what this answer rests on, because Title 33 changes often.

- **Anchored to the live PortlandMaps record (verified today, May 4, 2026):** zoning (R7 base + "h" overlay), absence of plan district / historic district / environmental overlay / flood / landslide / liquefaction zones, sewer connection status, lot dimensions, year built, stormwater basin, soil group, and slope.
- **Anchored to Portland.gov pages I just checked** (not training data): the existence and structure of the ADU SDC Waiver Program; the temporary SDC exemption window of Aug 15, 2025 through Sept 30, 2028 (under PCC 17.14.070 Section J / ordinance 192082); the THOW rules (33.260, 33.266.150) being live; the basic ADU framework (33.205) including the 800 sqft / 75% rule; the PP&D phone number and address.
- **Cited from Title 33 / Title 24 / 25 / 26 / 27 / Title 11 framework but not freshly read line-by-line:** the exact numerical setbacks, FAR, and lot coverage tables in 33.110 (Tables 110-3, 110-4, 110-5). The R7 numbers I gave (35 ft height, 10/5/10 setbacks, ~50% coverage) are typical and broadly correct, but the precise live numbers change with each code update packet. **Read the live PDF at portland.gov/code/33/100s/110 before designing to a specific dimension.**
- **Anything about RIP unit counts, minimum density, and the Shelter to Housing Continuum tweaks** — the broad strokes are correct (up to 4 units in R7 by right; 6 with 50% affordable; THOWs allowed since Aug 2021) but specific thresholds for minimum density, design standards for fourplexes, and the "constrained sites" overlay carve-outs all need a planner check on your specific lot.
- **Things I did NOT verify and that you should confirm:**
  1. Whether any street tree on SE 174th Ave's right-of-way frontage of your lot is currently regulated (Urban Forestry).
  2. Whether your existing 1947 house has any open code violations or unpermitted alterations on file (PP&D research). The 2009 nuisance complaint shows as Closed, so that's clear.
  3. Whether the existing 6" sewer lateral has documented capacity for an additional dwelling unit.
  4. Whether there are any recorded easements affecting the buildable area of your lot beyond the standard frontage utility easements (a title report will show this).
  5. Whether ownership records correctly reflect the current legal owner.

My honest recommendation: take this answer to the free 15-minute city planner appointment. Have them confirm the live numbers and flag anything I've gotten stale. That conversation costs nothing and is the single best dollar (well, no dollars) you can spend before paying anyone to draw plans.

If you'd like, I can save this as a markdown file you can drop into your Obsidian vault under `project-docs/` and update as you collect verified numbers from the city. Just say the word.

atoms.claim_full: Portland treats tiny houses on wheels as occupied recreational vehicles, allowing one on a developed residential lot already containing a primary dwelling.
